column5: Notes
Legendary founder of the dynasty; semi-mythical figure who ruled c. 705-675 BCE in Persis region; name means "having a friend's mind"
King of Anshan; expanded the territory; divided his kingdom between his sons - Cyrus I received Anshan and Ariaramnes received Persis
King of Anshan; mentioned in Babylonian texts as "king of Parsumash"; maintained independence from Median Empire
Ruled Persis region; mentioned in his inscriptions as "Great King, King of Kings"
Grandfather of Darius I; maintained control of Persis; contemporary with Cyrus II
Vassal king under Median rule; marriage to Mandane was a political alliance with the Medes

column2: Cassandane (principal wife, Achaemenid), Amytis of Media (political marriage, daughter of Astyages), Nitetis (Egyptian princess, disputed)
column3: Cambyses II, Bardiya (Smerdis), Atossa, Roxane, Artystone, Hystaspes (mentioned by some sources)
column4: Killed in battle (530 BCE against the Massagetae tribe led by Queen Tomyris)
column5: Founder of the Persian Empire (550-530 BCE); conquered Media, Lydia, Babylonia; referred to as "King of the Four Corners of the World"; issued Cyrus Cylinder; allowed Jews to return to Jerusalem
Principal wife of Cyrus; highly respected; Cyrus reportedly mourned her death publicly
Daughter of Median King Astyages; marriage sealed alliance; some historians question this marriage

column2: Phaidyme (daughter of Otanes), Atossa (his sister, disputed), Roxane (his sister, disputed), Nitetis (Egyptian princess, disputed)
column4: Death in Syria (522 BCE) - either suicide, accident, or assassination
column5: Ruled 530-522 BCE; conquered Egypt (525 BCE); implemented dual Egyptian-Persian titulature; portrayed negatively by Greek historians; called himself "King of Egypt"

column2: Phaidyme (widow of Cambyses II), several unnamed wives
column4: Assassinated (522 BCE by Darius I and six conspirators)
column5: Brief reign in 522 BCE; possibly an impostor named Gaumata according to Darius' Behistun Inscription; controversy remains over his true identity
Daughter of nobleman Otanes; helped expose the alleged "False Smerdis" (Gaumata) according to Herodotus
Persian nobleman; one of the seven conspirators against "False Smerdis"; father-in-law to royal family members

column3: Daughter who married Darius I, Mardonius (general)
column5: Persian nobleman; one of the seven conspirators against "False Smerdis"; father-in-law to Darius I

column2: Atossa (daughter of Cyrus II, chief wife), Artystone (daughter of Cyrus II), Parmys (daughter of Bardiya), Phratagune (daughter of Artanes), daughter of Gobryas, daughter of Otanes
column3: Xerxes I, Artobazanes, Masistes, Hystaspes, Achaemenes, Ariamenes, Arsames, Gobryas II, Abrocomes, Hyperantes, Artazostre, Mandane, Rhodogune
column5: Ruled 522-486 BCE; reorganized empire into satrapies; standardized currency (daric); expanded empire; built Persepolis; implemented Royal Road postal system; invaded Greece (First Persian War)

column2: Cambyses II (brother, disputed), Bardiya (brother, disputed), Darius I
column5: Extremely influential queen; ensured her son Xerxes became king despite not being firstborn; mentioned by Herodotus as having significant political power
Favorite wife of Darius according to Herodotus; had a golden statue made of her
Marriage legitimized Darius' claim to throne by connecting him to Cyrus II's family
Royal wife; Artanes was Darius' brother
Eldest son of Darius I; lost succession to Xerxes I
Son of Darius I; satrap of Bactria; killed in revolt against Xerxes

column2: Amestris (principal wife, daughter of Otanes), Esther (Biblical figure, disputed/legendary), Vashti (Biblical figure, disputed/legendary)
column3: Artaxerxes I, Darius, Hystaspes, Achaemenes, Rhodogune, Amytis
column4: Assassinated (465 BCE by Artabanus, commander of the royal bodyguard)
column5: Ruled 486-465 BCE; suppressed Egyptian revolt; expanded Persepolis; defeated at Salamis and Plataea; known for crossing the Hellespont with massive bridges of boats

column3: Artaxerxes I, Darius, Hystaspes, Achaemenes, Rhodogune, Amytis
column5: Known for her cruelty in Herodotus' accounts; powerful queen mother during Artaxerxes I's early reign

column2: Damaspia (chief wife), Cosmartidene (Babylonian), Alogyne (concubine), Andia (concubine), numerous others
column3: Xerxes II (by Damaspia), Sogdianus (by Alogyne), Darius II/Ochus (by Cosmartidene), Parysatis (by Andia), Arsites, Bagapaeus, others
column5: Ruled 465-424 BCE; known as "Artaxerxes Longimanus" for his long right hand; faced revolts in Egypt; maintained peace with Greece (Peace of Callias); Jewish tradition associates him with events in Book of Ezra

column4: Died same day as Artaxerxes I (424 BCE)
column5: Principal wife; her son was the legitimate heir

column4: Murdered (424 BCE, by his half-brother Sogdianus)
column5: Ruled only 45 days in 424 BCE; killed while drunk after a festival
Illegitimate son of Artaxerxes I; ruled for 6-7 months; killed by suffocation in ashes

column3: Artaxerxes II, Cyrus the Younger, Artostes, Oxendras, Ostanes
column5: Ruled 423-404 BCE; born illegitimate as "Ochus"; gained throne through violence; dealt with numerous revolts; heavily influenced by his wife Parysatis

column3: Artaxerxes II, Cyrus the Younger, Artostes, Oxendras, Ostanes
column5: Extremely powerful and ambitious queen; favored her son Cyrus over Artaxerxes; infamous for cruelty and revenge against enemies

column4: Killed in battle (401 BCE at Cunaxa)
column5: Satrap of Lydia; rebelled against brother Artaxerxes II; subject of Xenophon's "Anabasis"

column2: Stateira I (chief wife), Tiridatis, Cosmartidene, over 300 concubines according to Plutarch
column3: Darius (crown prince), Ariaspes, Artaxerxes III (Ochus), Atossa (daughter-wife), Apama, Sisygambis, Amestris, Rhodogune, Ocha, over 115 sons according to ancient sources
column5: Ruled 404-358 BCE; longest-reigning Achaemenid king; faced Cyrus' rebellion; lost Egypt; portrayed in Plutarch's biography; known as "Mnemon" (The Mindful)
Murdered by Cyrus' mother Parysatis after the Battle of Cunaxa
Crown prince who conspired against his father; executed with 50 co-conspirators
Second son of Artaxerxes II; driven to suicide by his brother Ochus

column2: Artaxerxes II (her father), later Artaxerxes III
column5: Daughter-wife of Artaxerxes II; later married her brother Artaxerxes III; suffered from illness according to Plutarch

column3: Artaxerxes IV (Arses), Bisthanes, several other sons executed by Bagoas
column4: Poisoned (338 BCE by the eunuch Bagoas)
column5: Ruled 358-338 BCE; ruthless; executed numerous relatives to secure throne; reconquered Egypt; crushed rebellions; poisoned with his sons by Bagoas
Ruled 338-336 BCE as a puppet of Bagoas; attempted to poison Bagoas but failed

column4: Forced to drink poison by Darius III (336 BCE)
column5: Powerful court eunuch and kingmaker; not royal blood but effective ruler during Artaxerxes IV's reign

column4: Killed by his cousin Bessus (330 BCE)
column5: Ruled 336-330 BCE; last Achaemenid king; defeated by Alexander the Great at Issus and Gaugamela; abandoned at Ecbatana

column4: Died in childbirth or illness (333 BCE)
column5: Renowned for beauty; captured by Alexander after Battle of Issus; treated with respect

column4: Possibly murdered after Alexander's death (323 BCE)
column5: Daughter of Darius III; married Alexander in 324 BCE at Susa Weddings

column4: Possibly murdered after Alexander's death (323 BCE)
column5: Younger daughter of Darius III; married at Susa Weddings
Young son of Darius III; mentioned in histories but fate unrecorded
Satrap of Bactria; killed Darius III; proclaimed himself king as "Artaxerxes V"; captured and executed by Alexander

column4: Suicide by starvation after Alexander's death (323 BCE)
column5: Mother of Darius III; captured after Battle of Issus; respected by Alexander; chose death upon hearing of Alexander's death
Brother of Darius III; fought at Issus and Gaugamela; later joined Alexander's entourage

column1: Dates are approximate as ancient Persian chronologies have inconsistencies
Some relationships, particularly in the early dynasty, are semi-legendary
Biblical connections (Esther, Vashti, etc.) are mentioned but disputed by historians
Many consorts and children, especially of later kings with large harems, are not recorded by name in historical sources
Ancient sources sometimes conflict on genealogical details
